add.question: Why does a reverse sear produce more even doneness than a traditional high-heat sear first?

add.reason: Explains why you cannot rush a braise -- connects muscle protein, collagen, and cooking method in one conceptual question.

add.question: How does glutamate (MSG) activate umami taste receptors differently from salt activating saltiness?
add.answer: Glutamate binds to T1R1/T1R3 taste receptors specifically tuned to detect free amino acids signaling protein-rich food; sodium activates entirely different ion-channel based receptors
choices: Glutamate binds to T1R1/T1R3 taste receptors specifically tuned to detect free amino acids signaling protein-rich food; sodium activates entirely different ion-channel based receptors
Glutamate and salt both work on the same sodium receptor pathway
MSG amplifies the saltiness signal rather than creating a new taste
Glutamate works by blocking bitter receptors, making food taste richer by contrast
add.reason: Expert-level taste receptor biology for the umami pathway -- genuinely specialist knowledge about why umami is a distinct fifth taste.

add.question: What is 'the water holding capacity paradox' in pale soft exudative (PSE) pork?
add.answer: Rapid post-slaughter pH drop while muscle temperature is still high causes protein denaturation, collapsing the protein network and releasing water, producing pale, soft, wet meat
choices: Rapid post-slaughter pH drop while muscle temperature is still high causes protein denaturation, collapsing the protein network and releasing water, producing pale, soft, wet meat
PSE pork results from the animal being over-hydrated before slaughter
High pH in the muscle causes proteins to over-retain water and swell
PSE pork is caused by excess fat content diluting the muscle protein network
add.reason: Expert meat science -- teaches the pH-temperature interaction that causes PSE, connecting food science to muscle biochemistry.

add.question: What does 'knife steel' (honing rod) actually do to a blade edge at the microscopic level?
add.answer: It realigns the microscopic teeth of the blade edge that fold over during use, restoring sharpness without removing metal
choices: It realigns the microscopic teeth of the blade edge that fold over during use, restoring sharpness without removing metal
It grinds away a thin layer of metal to expose a fresh sharp edge
It polishes the blade surface to reduce friction during cutting
It magnetizes the blade edge to attract and hold food particles
add.reason: Practical and conceptually interesting knife care question teaching what honing actually does -- fills a gap in the knife skills questions.

add.question: Why does dry-aging beef develop a more intense flavor than fresh or wet-aged beef?
add.answer: Enzymatic breakdown of proteins and fats produces amino acids and short-chain fatty acids; moisture evaporation concentrates these flavors further
choices: Enzymatic breakdown of proteins and fats produces amino acids and short-chain fatty acids; moisture evaporation concentrates these flavors further
Dry aging allows beneficial mold to grow that creates new flavor compounds
Bacteria on the surface produce fermented flavors that penetrate the meat
Oxygen exposure causes controlled oxidation that creates deep beefy flavors
add.reason: Fills a gap in the meat aging questions -- explains the enzyme and concentration mechanism behind dry-aged flavor development.

add.question: What is 'the science of mayonnaise stability' -- why does it stay emulsified?
add.answer: Lecithin in egg yolk has hydrophilic and hydrophobic ends that coat oil droplets, keeping them suspended in the water phase indefinitely when properly made
choices: Lecithin in egg yolk has hydrophilic and hydrophobic ends that coat oil droplets, keeping them suspended in the water phase indefinitely when properly made
The high viscosity of the oil prevents separation by slowing droplet movement
Mustard proteins cross-link with egg proteins to form a rigid network
Salt lowers the water activity enough to prevent phase separation
add.reason: Deep-dives into mayonnaise emulsion chemistry beyond the basic emulsification definition already present in the pool.

add.question: Why does refrigerating potatoes cause them to taste sweeter?
add.answer: Cold temperatures activate phosphorylase enzymes that convert starch into sugars -- a process called cold-induced sweetening
choices: Cold temperatures activate phosphorylase enzymes that convert starch into sugars -- a process called cold-induced sweetening
Refrigeration causes water inside the potato to partially freeze, concentrating natural sugars
Cold slows respiration, allowing more sugar to accumulate from outside sources
Low temperature breaks down the cell walls, releasing trapped sucrose
add.reason: Interesting and counterintuitive food science -- cold-induced sweetening of potatoes is a specific enzymatic reaction worth knowing.

add.question: Why does cooking garlic whole, sliced, and minced produce different levels of pungency?
add.answer: More cell damage releases more alliinase enzyme to convert alliin into allicin -- mincing produces the most, whole cloves the least pungency
choices: More cell damage releases more alliinase enzyme to convert alliin into allicin -- mincing produces the most, whole cloves the least pungency
Heat destroys pungency equally regardless of cut size
Larger pieces retain more sulfur compounds because less surface area is exposed to air
The outer membrane of garlic cells contains the pungent compounds, so peeling releases them
add.reason: Teaches the alliin-alliinase enzyme system in garlic -- explains a practical observation every cook has made about different cuts of garlic.

add.question: What is 'the stall' in barbecue low-and-slow cooking of large cuts of meat?
add.answer: The internal temperature plateaus around 65-75 degC as evaporative cooling from surface moisture exactly balances the heat input
choices: The internal temperature plateaus around 65-75 degC as evaporative cooling from surface moisture exactly balances the heat input
The meat temporarily stops absorbing smoke flavor at high internal temperature
Fat renders suddenly, flooding the meat and temporarily cooling it
Connective tissue absorbs heat energy as it converts to gelatin during this period
add.reason: Explains the physics of evaporative cooling in barbecue -- a very practical concept for pitmasters that involves thermodynamics.

add.question: Why does adding a pinch of salt to chocolate desserts enhance chocolate flavor?
add.answer: Salt suppresses the bitter taste receptors that respond to cocoa's polyphenols, while umami-like glutamate interactions make the chocolate flavor seem richer and more rounded
choices: Salt suppresses the bitter taste receptors that respond to cocoa's polyphenols, while umami-like glutamate interactions make the chocolate flavor seem richer and more rounded
Salt chemically reacts with cocoa solids to release additional flavor compounds
Salt raises the melting point of cocoa butter, intensifying chocolate mouthfeel
Salt masks any remaining dairy off-flavors from milk chocolate
add.reason: Practical technique tip grounded in taste receptor physiology -- explains the salt-in-chocolate phenomenon many bakers use intuitively.

add.question: What is 'fermentation temperature control' critical for in producing different beer styles?
add.answer: Lower temperatures favor clean lager yeast character; higher temperatures produce esters and fusel alcohols from ale yeasts, creating fruity or spicy notes
choices: Lower temperatures favor clean lager yeast character; higher temperatures produce esters and fusel alcohols from ale yeasts, creating fruity or spicy notes
Temperature only affects the speed of fermentation, not the flavor
Higher temperature is always better for yeast activity and beer flavor
Fermentation temperature must always be exactly 20 degC for any yeast
add.reason: Connects fermentation temperature to yeast metabolism and ester production -- important for understanding flavor development in fermented foods.

add.question: What is 'the leavening mechanism of baking powder' -- specifically a double-acting powder?
add.answer: Double-acting baking powder releases CO2 twice: once when wet (from tartrate salts) and again when heated (from sodium aluminum sulfate), ensuring leavening continues in the oven
choices: Double-acting baking powder releases CO2 twice: once when wet (from tartrate salts) and again when heated (from sodium aluminum sulfate), ensuring leavening continues in the oven
Baking powder releases all its CO2 at once when it contacts moisture
Double-acting refers to it working in both baking and frying applications
The two actions are leavening and flavoring -- it releases CO2 and neutralizes acid
add.reason: Fills a gap on the chemistry of chemical leavening -- the double-acting mechanism is widely used but rarely understood.

add.question: What happens to capsaicin when cream or full-fat milk is consumed after eating very spicy food?
add.answer: Casein protein in dairy binds to and strips capsaicin from TRPV1 receptors, providing relief -- water cannot do this as capsaicin is not water-soluble
choices: Casein protein in dairy binds to and strips capsaicin from TRPV1 receptors, providing relief -- water cannot do this as capsaicin is not water-soluble
The cold temperature of dairy numbs the pain receptors temporarily
Dairy fat dissolves the capsaicin molecules, diluting them chemically
The alkalinity of milk neutralizes capsaicin's acidic properties
add.reason: Teaches capsaicin's oil-solubility and casein's role as a detergent -- explains a universal experience with a clear biochemical mechanism.

add.question: What is the 'smoke point' dependent on chemically in an oil?
add.answer: Free fatty acid content -- more free fatty acids (from oxidation or impurities) lower the smoke point; refined oils have fewer free fatty acids and higher smoke points
choices: Free fatty acid content -- more free fatty acids (from oxidation or impurities) lower the smoke point; refined oils have fewer free fatty acids and higher smoke points
The degree of saturation -- saturated fats always have higher smoke points
The viscosity of the oil -- thicker oils smoke at higher temperatures
The color of the oil -- lighter oils always have higher smoke points
add.reason: Explains the chemistry behind smoke point differences -- free fatty acid content is the key variable, not the type of fat. Fills a conceptual gap.
